F*ck The Hype - Why You Shouldn't Use OpenClaw
Ok. I know the title of this article is ironic, coming from someone who has built a large chunk of their AI workflows around OpenClaw. I've posted about it every single week. I literally have a content series called "Day X of building my AI workforce."
And I'm still telling you - most of you shouldn't be using it.
Before you come for me in the replies, hear me out. This isn't an anti-OpenClaw article. This is anti-hype. Too many creators are trend-jacking OpenClaw for engagement without telling you the truth. And that is: For most people, there are better alternatives right now. And the landscape just shifted dramatically in the last week.

The Dirty Secret The Hype Beasts Don't Talk About

Here's what actually happens to 90% of people who get into OpenClaw:
You see the viral tweets. You buy a Mac Mini. You install OpenClaw. You spend a weekend configuring agents. You feel like a genius for about 48 hours.
Then you realise you have no idea what to actually automate.
Your workflows break. Your agents hallucinate. You spend more time debugging than working. And now you have a $1,000+ machine sitting on your desk doing the job of a $20/month subscription.
I've seen this play out dozens of times in DMs (and anecdotally with my friends/employees). The tool isn't the problem. The approach is.
But here's what nobody in the OpenClaw space is paying attention to. While they were debugging agent configs, Anthropic, Notion, and others just dropped a wave of announcements that change the entire equation.

What Just Dropped (And Why It Changes Everything)

In the last few weeks alone, we've had a wave of announcements that genuinely change the calculus on whether OpenClaw makes sense for most people.
Let me walk through them: 1. Claude Code - Remote Control (Mobile) Anthropic launched a mobile version of Claude Code called Remote Control. You scan a QR code from your terminal, and you can now control Claude Code from your iPhone or Android.
No Mac Mini. No VPS. No server. No terminal open on your desk. You send a task from your phone, and Claude builds it in the background.
One of the big value props of Openclaw was its accessibility via Telegram/Whatsapp/Discord - this now mitigates part of that for a lot of users.
Claude
@claudeai
·
Feb 24
New in Claude Code: Remote Control. Kick off a task in your terminal and pick it up from your phone while you take a walk or join a meeting. Claude keeps running on your machine, and you can control the session from the Claude app or http://claude.ai/code
2. Claude Cowork Business Updates If Claude Code is for developers, Cowork is for everyone else. It's a GUI-based agent that does real work - not just answering questions, but executing multi-step tasks across your actual tools.
They just added integrations with Slack, Figma, Canva, Box, and Clay. Plus, they've rolled out industry-specific plugins for financial services, HR, design, and private equity.
When Anthropic dropped the financial plugins, a software industry ETF fell 6% in a single day. When they launched Claude Code Security on Feb 20, cybersecurity stocks crashed the same afternoon.
That should tell you everything about how seriously the market is taking this.
For 80% of what most people are trying to do with OpenClaw (research, file management, content workflows, data analysis), the truth is: Cowork already does it.
Claude
@claudeai
·
Feb 24
Introducing Cowork and plugin updates that help enterprises customize Claude for better collaboration with every team.
3. Notion Agents This one flew under the radar, but it shouldn't have (especially if you're a Notion user like me).
Notion rebuilt their entire AI as autonomous agents. These aren't chatbots. They execute multi-step workflows autonomously for 20+ minutes. They have memory. They connect to Slack, Google Drive, GitHub. You can set them on schedules and triggers.
For knowledge work - project management, meeting prep, research, content planning, database management - Notion Agents are already better than most people's OpenClaw setups. And the barrier to entry is literally zero.
If your primary use case for OpenClaw was "organise my business and automate my workflows," honestly, this is a good place to start.
Notion
@NotionHQ
·
Feb 24
Introducing Custom Agents. The AI team that never sleeps They’re autonomous, built for teams, and easy for anyone to build. Give them a job, set a trigger or schedule, and they'll get it done 'round the clock.
4. Manus / n8n / Zapier I'm not going to spend too long on these (I have future content coming which does deeper). But it's worth stating the obvious: for basic automation - email scraping, web research, SOP generation, lead enrichment - these tools work today.
If you haven't maxed out what these can do for you, you probably have no business buying a Mac Mini.

The Scalability Problem Nobody Mentions

There's also a scalability issue that the OpenClaw community glosses over.
Claude Code scales infinitely in the cloud. More compute, more parallel tasks, more power - it grows with you. OpenClaw runs on your hardware. When you hit the ceiling, your only option is to buy another Mac Mini.
And it's not just scale. Claude Code plugs directly into GitHub, VS Code, and Xcode through MCP. They just shipped security scanning, lifecycle hooks, hot reloading, and session handoffs between devices. The ecosystem is expanding weekly.
For most people, cloud-based tools are simply more practical.

Where OpenClaw Still Wins

Now, before you think I've completely switched sides - let me be clear about where OpenClaw is still unmatched.
  1. Full local control. Your data never leaves your machine. For anyone handling sensitive business data, client information, or proprietary workflows, this matters.
  2. Complex multi-agent orchestration. Running 5 specialised agents that talk to each other, delegate tasks, and operate as a coordinated system - cloud tools can't match this yet. This is where OpenClaw is genuinely ahead of everything else (the #1 reason I'm still using it).
  3. Custom agent personalities. SOUL files, detailed configuration, agents that understand your business context deeply - this level of customisation doesn't exist anywhere else right now.
  4. 24/7 autonomous operation. Once it's set up properly, your agents run around the clock without subscription costs eating into your margins. Over time, the economics actually favour OpenClaw - if you've done the work upfront.
  5. True ownership. You own the entire stack. Especially if you're running local models.
If you've already invested the time to build a proper OpenClaw setup with real, validated use cases, you're still in a powerful position. But here's how I'm thinking of Openclaw now, in the wake of all the new updates that are shipping industry-wide: It is a great tool, but it's not the ONLY tool. I'm using Claude Code for specific builds/workflows. I'm using Notion agents for business automations. I'm even using GPT for strategy. Imo, there is no all-in-one solution. The best thing to do is use specific tools for specific purposes. Where Openclaw is particularly useful for me is automated scraping and autonomous product iteration. But it's an extremely personal thing.

So What Should You Actually Do?

If you're starting from zero, here are my honest recommendations:
Step 1: Start with Claude (Cowork or Code, depending on your technical level). Get familiar with what AI agents can actually do for your specific workflow. This is the best place to start for 99% of people imo.
Step 2: Add Notion Agents and/or Manus/n8n for your knowledge work and basic automations. Test what's worth automating and what isn't. Low-stakes way to test new workflows.
Step 3: Once you've genuinely outgrown these tools (or notice a clear gap none of your existing workflows can automate) - that's when OpenClaw makes sense. Because now you know exactly what you need it to do.
Most people are starting at Step 3 and wondering why nothing works.

The Bottom Line

OpenClaw is great for some people, and is genuinely worth experimenting with if you want to be on the cutting edge of AI.
BUT, the hype cycle has convinced people that buying hardware and configuring agents is the path to AI leverage. It's not. The path is to understand what to automate first, test with accessible tools, and only graduate to OpenClaw when you truly need it.
I still use OpenClaw every day. I still believe in it. But I'd be doing you a disservice if I pretended it's the right starting point for everyone.
Start with the tools above. Outgrow them. Then build the machine.
That's the order. Most people have it backwards.
